spinoffs.title: A. RECENT SPINOFFS
spinoffs.blurb: US spin-offs from the last 12-24 months

items.name: Curbline Properties Corp.
items.thesis: First pure-play 'convenience real estate' REIT spun from SITE Centers with ~$500M net cash and a long runway to deploy into a fragmented ~$70k-property niche.
items.why: Optically expensive at ~23x AFFO until you adjust for the massive net cash position used to fund accretive acquisitions; spin from a struggling shopping-center REIT (SITC) means most legacy holders dumped without ever assessing the new vehicle. Limited sell-side coverage and small float keep it off most institutional screens.
items.edge: CURB is acquiring convenience centers at high-cap-rate spreads to its cost of equity and sits on dry powder representing roughly 17% of its market cap; AFFO/share growth runway should re-rate the multiple as cash gets put to work and net debt-funded growth amplifies returns. Owns just 78 of ~68,678 US convenience properties — TAM is enormous.
catalysts: Inclusion in REIT indices once seasoned

items.name: South Bow Corporation
items.thesis: Pure-play Keystone liquids pipeline spun from TC Energy with ~94% contracted cash flows and a ~7% dividend yield — orphan stock for both Canadian dividend funds and US energy MLP benchmarks.
items.why: Canadian-domiciled spin trades on NYSE without index inclusion, creating dual orphan-status; many TRP holders sold on receipt because they wanted natural-gas/regulated-utility exposure not crude pipelines. Keystone segment carries lingering safety/litigation overhang from prior leaks that obscures the contracted earnings stream.
items.edge: Market underweights how heavily contracted (~94% recurring) revenues are post-Keystone XL settlement; Canadian heavy-crude supply growth into Gulf Coast refiners is structurally underserved with very few competing pipes. As an obscure spin, every quarter of clean operations should compress the discount to FCF peers like Plains All American.
catalysts: Potential Blackrod or Hardisty terminal expansions
Settlement of remaining historical leak liabilities
Re-rating as MLP-style yield investors discover it

items.name: Everus Construction Group, Inc.
items.thesis: Specialty electrical/mechanical contractor spun from MDU at a backlog levered to the data-center & grid-modernization capex super-cycle.
items.why: MDU is held by utility/dividend funds that immediately sold the cyclical construction stub; sell-side coverage was sparse at spin. Original spin valuation models assumed peer multiples of ~14x EBITDA, but the data-center mix has accelerated faster than expected.
items.edge: Backlog has grown to $2.8B with mid-single-digit EBITDA margin expansion as data-center work mix rises; ECG is becoming a pure-play 'pick-and-shovel' on hyperscaler buildouts that the market is still pricing as a regional electrical contractor. Insider buying and modest analyst initiation underway.
catalysts: Quarterly backlog disclosures showing data-center mix
Potential M&A using clean balance sheet

items.name: Magnera Corporation
items.thesis: World's largest nonwovens producer formed by spinning Berry's HHNF business and merging with Glatfelter — micro-cap orphan trading at <3x EBITDA with a self-help margin story.
items.why: Berry shareholders received only ~10% of their value in Magnera shares and sold indiscriminately given micro-cap size and low yield. Heavy debt (~$1.7B net) on $370M EBITDA scares quality investors, and Glatfelter's prior operational issues leave a stigma. No sell-side coverage from the major banks.
items.edge: Pro-forma synergies from the Berry/Glatfelter combination are only ~30% realized; merger cost-out plus working capital release should drive FCF inflection in 2026 that the market hasn't underwritten. EV/EBITDA <6x vs. specialty packaging peers at 9-11x; even modest deleveraging double-digit equity re-rate is plausible.
catalysts: Refinancing of post-spin debt at lower coupon
First analyst initiations from packaging coverage teams

items.name: Concentra Group Holdings Parent, Inc.
items.thesis: #1 US occupational-health platform (549 centers + 156 onsite clinics) spun from Select Medical with a dominant share in employer-paid workers'-comp clinics.
items.why: SEM was held primarily by inpatient-rehab investors who sold the outpatient occ-health stub; IPO/spin combination structure (June IPO then November distribution) caused two waves of forced selling. Boring B2B niche means little retail interest and limited bulge-bracket coverage relative to size.
items.edge: DOT physical and workers'-comp exam mandates create utility-like recurring volume immune to economic cycles; Concentra has pricing power and consolidation runway. ~$1.8B revenue with mid-teens margins yet trades at high-single-digit EBITDA multiple vs healthcare-services peers at 11-13x.
catalysts: M&A roll-up of independent occ-health clinics

items.name: Amentum Holdings, Inc.
items.thesis: Government-services giant created from Jacobs CMS+C&I spin merged with legacy Amentum — $14.4B revenue, $47B backlog, deleveraging story masking strong recurring federal cash flows.
items.why: Created via Reverse Morris Trust merger with Lindsay Goldberg/American Securities's PE-backed Amentum, leaving overhang from PE selldowns and confused pro-forma financials. Net leverage of ~4.0x at spin scared off mainstream defense investors who prefer LDOS/SAIC's cleaner balance sheets.
items.edge: Net leverage already cut from 4.0x to 3.2x in fiscal 2025 with management targeting <2.5x by 2027; valuation gap to peers (LDOS, SAIC, BAH) is ~3-4 EBITDA turns purely on debt overhang that is rapidly resolving. Backlog book-to-bill consistently >1.0x. Truist target $42 (~50% upside).

items.name: Seaport Entertainment Group Inc.
items.thesis: Micro-cap stub of Howard Hughes' 'orphan' NYC + Las Vegas entertainment assets (Seaport, Tropicana air rights, Las Vegas Aviators) trading well below sum-of-parts value of underlying real estate.
items.why: Pershing/Bill Ackman's HHH holders received 1 SEG per 9 HHH and immediately dumped — too small for institutions and economically diluted. NYSE-American listing means no S&P inclusion. Operating losses obscure ~$700M+ of unique NYC/Vegas RE that took decades to assemble.
items.edge: Tropicana Las Vegas air rights and the Seaport district have Class-A locations that are essentially impossible to replicate; market values these as op-co losses while a real-estate sum-of-parts (especially with rights-offering capital deployed) suggests $40-50/share NAV. Activist/strategic interest possible given micro-cap size and trophy assets.
catalysts: Tropicana site joint venture or sale
Las Vegas Aviators stadium economics improvement
Potential acquisition by HHH or 3rd party at NAV

items.name: Millrose Properties, Inc.
items.thesis: First publicly traded 'land-banking REIT' spun from Lennar with $5.5B contributed land + $1B cash, 142k controlled homesites, generating contracted option-fee yield from Lennar takedowns.
items.why: Lennar holders are home-builder equity investors with no appetite for an externally-managed REIT — forced selling on receipt was acute. Externally managed by Kennedy Lewis carries fee-drag stigma. Novel business model (recycled land bank) lacks direct comps so multiples are anchored low.
items.edge: Contracted takedowns from Lennar provide highly predictable fee revenue with limited entitlement/development risk, more akin to a finance company than a land speculator; market is mis-pricing this as a homebuilder rather than a 10-12% ROE recurring-fee compounder. BTIG initiated Buy with $35 PT calling it 'cheap to opportunity'. Lennar still owns 20% so interests aligned.
catalysts: First full year of homesite-takedown reporting

items.name: Sandisk Corporation
items.thesis: Pure-play NAND flash leader spun from WDC at a NAND cycle bottom; only US-listed pure-NAND vehicle and a primary beneficiary of AI data-center NAND demand explosion.
items.why: Despite the run from ~$28 lows to ~$920+, the market still arguably underwrites a normal-cycle NAND business; structural AI-driven shortage with bit-demand growth (~22%) outpacing supply (~16%) and contract-NAND prices up 85-90% q/q creates an extended supercycle. Some legacy WDC holders still dumping into strength.
items.edge: Joint development with SK Hynix on 'High Bandwidth Flash' (HBF) targeting 8-16x HBM capacity at comparable bandwidth could be the next NVIDIA-adjacent infrastructure trade; Sandisk has the consumer-brand and IP to capture this. Even at current price, forward EV/EBITDA still discounts cycle peak.

items.name: Angi Inc.
items.thesis: Home-services marketplace finally separated from IAC's holding-company complexity; new CEO + cleaner cap table + low expectations create classic small-cap spin set-up.
items.why: Angi was held at IAC purely for optionality and the spin freed Diller to focus IAC on other bets — IAC holders sold without a second thought. Years of self-inflicted operational issues (lead quality, pro churn) make most investors leave it for dead. Limited float and micro-cap status.
items.edge: New management has rationalized the marketplace, killed unprofitable cohorts and is now showing quality-adjusted growth; trades at <0.5x revenue with positive FCF and ~$300M of cash on a $320M market cap (effectively trading at enterprise value near zero). Optionality on AI-driven matching efficiency is essentially free.
catalysts: Sentiment reset as standalone reporting begins

items.name: Starz Entertainment Corp.
items.thesis: Premium subscription streamer (20M subs, 70% digital) finally separated from the studio business after years of delays; tiny float, M&A optionality, ~8x OIBDA.
items.why: Lionsgate holders wanted the studio, not a declining cable network; pre-spin rumors of distressed cap structure scared away buyers. Sub-$400M market cap is sub-scale for most institutions and benchmarks. Optical 'cable-network melting ice cube' bias.
items.edge: 70% of revenue is now digital and OIBDA more than doubled in Q4 2025 ($45.5M → $93.3M), evidence the digital pivot is working; CEO Jeffrey Hirsch has explicitly positioned STRZ as a streaming roll-up vehicle. At 1.2x trailing sales and ~8x OIBDA, the market hasn't recognized the model transition. Strategic buyers (PARA, WBD, AMC Networks) likely to circle.
catalysts: Quarterly digital sub adds and ARPU
Index inclusion as standalone seasoning completes

items.name: Ralliant Corporation
items.thesis: Precision-instruments segment spun from Fortive — slower-growth, more capital-intensive cousin orphaned by FTV holders, trading at ~13x FCF vs. industrial-test peers at 17-20x.
items.why: Fortive was a high-quality compounder owned by quality/growth funds; the slower-growth RAL stub was sold immediately. Tariff exposure and a mid-single-digit revenue decline guidance gave nervous holders cover to exit. Spin-off complexity around carve-out financials further obscures the real run-rate.
items.edge: Ralliant has 25% recurring revenue and dominant niche positions (Tektronix, Fluke, Sensing Technologies) that historically generate 20%+ EBITDA margins; trading at ~13x FCF when peers (KEYS, COHU, AME) trade 17-20x. Standalone capital allocation (buybacks, M&A) under new CEO can add meaningful value. VIC writeups suggest fair value $58-68.
catalysts: Investor day articulating long-term margin targets

items.name: Amrize Ltd.
items.thesis: Largest US/Canada cement, ready-mix and roofing supplier spun from Swiss parent Holcim — pure-play levered to US infrastructure, mega-projects, and reshoring capex.
items.why: Listed on both NYSE and SIX (Zurich); European parent's holders dumped on receipt because they want EU/EM exposure not US construction. US dual-listing creates index orphan-status (not in S&P, not in Stoxx). Initial coverage was lukewarm with hold ratings, leaving expectations low.
items.edge: $2T/yr US construction spend with $1.2T IIJA + CHIPS + IRA tailwinds is structurally favorable; #1 cement position with regional moats means pricing power in concrete shortage. Trading at ~9x EBITDA vs. CRH at 11x and pure-play US peers (EXP, MLM, SUM, VMC) at 13-18x — gap should close as standalone reporting and US analyst coverage catches up.
catalysts: First standalone investor day with US-focused targets
Potential cement plant capacity expansion announcements
Possible secondary listing changes / index inclusion

items.name: GCI Liberty, Inc. (Series A)
items.thesis: Alaska's monopoly broadband + wireless operator (~90% market share) spun from Liberty Broadband ahead of Charter/Cox merger; classic Liberty spin orphan with strong moat and modest expectations.
items.why: LBRDA holders only wanted Charter exposure, so most sold the GCI stub immediately; tracker-stock complexity and Series A/C/R confusion suppressed fair price discovery. Alaskan-only operator is an obscure niche outside the S&P benchmarks. PIPE structure tax treatment is unusual.
items.edge: GCI is essentially a regulated utility for Alaska connectivity with limited competitive threat (geography is the moat); trades at materially lower EBITDA multiple than peer broadband operators (Frontier, Windstream, WOW). Liberty management has consistently rewarded patient holders via tax-efficient structures and buybacks; a private equity take-private at peer multiples is a realistic upside scenario.
catalysts: Charter/Cox merger close removes complexity overhang
Potential PE take-private or strategic combination

items.name: Solstice Advanced Materials Inc.
items.thesis: Pure-play specialty materials (refrigerants, semi/electronic materials, advanced fibers) spun from Honeywell; ~$3.8B sales, 25%+ EBITDA margins, regulatory tailwinds in HFOs and AI-driven cooling demand.
items.why: Honeywell-style 'quality' holders weren't buying a chemicals carve-out; HON-to-SOLS distribution ratio (1:4) created small-position-size selling pressure. Looming HFO patent expirations are over-discounted by the market. Fresh BMO Outperform at $70 PT, but most generalist coverage is still missing.
items.edge: Refrigerants (72% of sales) are levered to data-center liquid cooling, EV thermal management and regulatory HFO->HFO blend transitions where SOLS has IP leadership; electronic materials segment touches every advanced-node semiconductor. Ability to fund next-gen refrigerants from cash flow before HFO commoditization is underappreciated. Forced selling is still settling.
catalysts: Q4 2025 / Q1 2026 first standalone earnings prints

items.name: Qnity Electronics, Inc.
items.thesis: Semiconductor & electronic-materials pure-play spun from DuPont (semi tech + interconnect solutions); 'picks-and-shovels' to AI accelerator and advanced-node semiconductor build-out.
items.why: DuPont generalist chemicals holders sold the higher-multiple electronics stub to fund continued ownership of the slower industrial chem parent; complex carve-out financials initially obscured run-rate margins. Despite a strong debut, pricing is still anchored to the spin-distribution price rather than peer multiples.
items.edge: Hidden gem inside DuPont — semiconductor materials franchise has 25%+ EBITDA margins, mid-teens revenue growth, and supplies consumables for thermal management/EMI shielding/advanced-node — directly levered to Nvidia/AMD/hyperscaler accelerator builds. Oppenheimer Outperform $100 PT; peer multiples (ENTG, CCMP, CBT) suggest 30%+ upside re-rating.
catalysts: Potential M&A or buybacks with clean balance sheet

items.name: The Magnum Ice Cream Company N.V.
items.thesis: Global #1 ice-cream pure-play (Magnum, Ben & Jerry's, Wall's, Breyers) demerged from Unilever; brand portfolio at consumer-staples valuation discount given trapped within slower CPG.
items.why: Unilever holders sold without ever opening a model — too small for the global CPG benchmarks they hold. SEC delays during US gov't shutdown caused timing confusion. Ben & Jerry's social-issue noise added headline risk that scares conservative consumer holders. NV / European structure adds friction for US institutional buyers.
items.edge: Standalone, MICC has 20%+ EBITDA margin potential vs. parent's diluted ~14%; pricing power on premium brands has been demonstrated through 2022-2024 inflation. Strategic suitors (Mondelez, Mars, Nestle) plus financial sponsors are obvious in $9B-$15B EV range. Recent pricing actions and supply-chain optimization haven't been re-rated.
catalysts: US dual listing or ADR re-listing for index inclusion

items.name: Versant Media Group, Inc.
items.thesis: Comcast's cable network portfolio (USA, MSNBC/MS NOW, CNBC, Syfy, E!, Golf, Oxygen) + Fandango/Rotten Tomatoes spun off; deeply discounted 'melting ice cube' that generated $1.4B net income in 2024.
items.why: Comcast holders are owning broadband + theme parks + Sky, didn't want the linear-cable shrinkage; spin opened down 14% on day one with indiscriminate selling. Linear TV pessimism is at peak — Versant projected to decline in 2025 — even though it generates >$1B FCF that funds buybacks/transformation. Tracking PARA/WBD secular compression makes investors gun-shy.
items.edge: Even with linear declines, the assets generate massive FCF and 80%+ revenue is still profitable; CEO Mark Lazarus has explicitly signaled M&A roll-up strategy. Likely active acquirer of CW, AMC Networks or Scripps assets. Trading at ~5x EBITDA / sub-1x revenue creates significant downside protection plus optionality on transformation or strategic combination.
catalysts: First quarterly FCF print as standalone
Take-private rumors (PE has shown interest in cable nets)

items.name: Atrium Therapeutics, Inc.
items.thesis: RNA-therapeutic spinout from Avidity-Novartis deal with $270M cash and 2 preclinical genetic-cardiomyopathy programs — trading near cash with all platform value free.
items.why: Created via Novartis acquisition of Avidity, Avidity holders received Atrium stock as consideration sweetener and most sold immediately to lock in Novartis cash. Preclinical biotech with no revenue gets minimal coverage. Inherited Avidity's old ticker (RNA) — confused traders who expected the parent's siRNA leadership think they are buying the wrong thing.
items.edge: $270M cash on $220M market cap means investors are paid to own the platform; Kathleen Gallagher (former Avidity CPO) brings real RNA-conjugate know-how. PLN cardiomyopathy and PRKAG2 syndrome have demonstrable orphan-disease economics, and Novartis would be a logical re-acquirer if either lead progresses. Asymmetric: small downside (cash floor), 5-10x upside on positive Phase 1 data.
catalysts: Potential partnership or acquisition by Novartis or other RNA-therapeutic player

items.name: Versigent PLC
items.thesis: Brand-new spin (April 1, 2026) of Aptiv's electrical-distribution-systems business — global leader in vehicle wiring/power architectures; classic 'too small to bother' setup right at maximum forced selling.
items.why: Spin completed less than 3 weeks ago — peak forced-selling window. Aptiv was held by tech/AI software/EV growth funds who wanted the high-multiple S&S/electronics business; the legacy wiring stub is anathema and being sold indiscriminately. Versigent inherited $2.1B of new debt at distribution, scaring quality-focused buyers.
items.edge: 100-year heritage as the dominant wiring-harness supplier with engineering centers on 4 continents and manufacturing in 30+ countries — duopoly economics with Yazaki/Sumitomo; this is critical-path content per vehicle and has structural growth as EV wiring complexity rises. CEO Joe Liotine has run the segment since 2024 and knows where margin can be added. Fresh spin, no analyst coverage yet — buy ahead of the initiation cycle.

items.name: GE Vernova Inc.
items.thesis: Pure-play energy/grid equipment giant spun from GE — power, wind, electrification segments — became the centerpiece of the AI-data-center grid build-out trade.
items.why: Originally spun at ~$141 with skepticism about wind losses and grid cyclicality; legacy GE holders rotated into Aerospace and PE/quality holders had no model for a power-generation cyclical. Market still anchors on early-2024 spin valuation while the order book has grown >$120B.
items.edge: Power and Electrification segments effectively own the choke point on grid transformer/turbine capacity for AI data-center build-out; pricing power is real and sustained — gas-turbine slots are sold out into 2028. Wind losses now narrowing while order backlog is locked in at much higher prices. Even after the run, forward EBITDA bridge supports continued upside on margin expansion alone.

items.name: Atmus Filtration Technologies, Inc.
items.thesis: Pure-play industrial filtration leader (Fleetguard brand) split off from Cummins via exchange offer — high-margin razor/razor-blade aftermarket model with consolidation runway in industrial air filtration.
items.why: Cummins holders received Atmus through an oversubscribed exchange offer that left an immediate float overhang; quality-defense holders avoided it as a 'commoditized filter business' until they ran the numbers. Long-term EV displacement risk is over-discounted relative to the 15-20 year tail of diesel fleets that need consumables.
items.edge: Recently acquired Koch Filter for $450M to enter $13B industrial air filtration TAM with much better growth profile; aftermarket revenue is ~80% of segment, recurring and pricing-led. 20% EBITDA margins, ~$160M FCF, 2.0x leverage post-deal — clean compounder. Buybacks and bolt-on M&A pipeline increasingly de-risk thesis.
catalysts: Continued bolt-on M&A in industrial air filtration

items.name: Inhibrx Biosciences, Inc.
items.thesis: Spun off from Inhibrx as part of Sanofi's $1.7B acquisition; received non-INBRX-101 pipeline (INBRX-109 chondrosarcoma, INBRX-1061) plus Sanofi/CVR economics; up 5x in 12 months and still under-followed.
items.why: Created via split-off transaction in conjunction with Sanofi M&A, leaving forced-seller dynamics from arbs and deal-funds; preclinical/early-clinical biotech with no revenue gets minimal sell-side coverage. Most former Inhibrx holders just took the Sanofi cash and CVRs and moved on.
items.edge: INBRX-109 (chondrosarcoma DR5 agonist) has shown strong early single-arm Phase 2 data in conventional chondrosarcoma — a rare cancer with no approved therapies; pivotal readout could trigger a buyout or accelerated approval. Core team is a proven biotech operator (former Inhibrx mgmt). Still small enough that single-asset success is transformative.

items.name: Sunrise Realty Trust, Inc.
items.thesis: Externally managed CRE-debt REIT focused on Sun-Belt commercial real estate loans, spun from cannabis lender AFC Gamma — micro-cap orphan with 14% dividend yield and clean balance sheet.
items.why: AFCG holders are cannabis-credit specialists who received a Sun-Belt CRE-mezz REIT they had zero appetite for; immediate forced selling. ~$110M market cap is too small for almost any institutional benchmark. Externally managed structure (Sunrise Manager LLC fee-drag) keeps quality investors away.
items.edge: Pure Sun-Belt CRE loan book at a time when distressed banks are exiting CRE lending — non-bank originators with capital can pick over the carcass at high spreads. Dividend yield ~14% covered by current loan-book interest income; loan portfolio is brand new (2024-2026 vintage), so credit risk is freshly underwritten. Tradable strategic alternatives review opens the door to liquidation/rollup at NAV.
catalysts: Loan-portfolio mark-to-market updates as Sun-Belt CRE stabilizes

items.name: Sirius XM Holdings Inc.
items.thesis: 'New Sirius' created by combining Liberty SiriusXM tracking stock with the public Sirius XM minority stake — clean cap-table FCF compounder yielding ~5% with Buffett's Berkshire owning ~35%.
items.why: Tracking-stock split-off + immediate merger created confused supply/demand on the new ticker; legacy Liberty holders sold tax-free and minorities received tiny stakes after a 1:10 conversion. Streaming-secular-decline narrative dominates despite stable subscriber base and strong FCF.
items.edge: Berkshire's continued accumulation (now ~35%) provides downside support and validates intrinsic-value thesis; clean post-split cap structure ($8.5B mcap) trades at <8x FCF with $1.2B annual FCF funding buybacks and ~5% dividend. Auto-attach business stabilizing as new vehicle deliveries pick up; podcast/ad-supported tier expanding TAM. Take-private optionality with Buffett at 35% is real.
catalysts: Quarterly FCF prints and capital returns

items.name: Brookfield Wealth Solutions Ltd.
items.thesis: Brookfield's insurance/annuity arm (formerly Brookfield Reinsurance) re-branded with new ticker after expanding via Just Group acquisition; share class exchangeable 1-for-1 into BN parent stock creates structural arbitrage.
items.why: Listed on both NYSE and TSX under same ticker as a Bermuda-domiciled exchangeable share — confuses US screens and excludes from Canadian-equity benchmarks. Insurance investors don't recognize it as a peer to Athene/Global Atlantic; Brookfield investors view it as a sub-economic appendage. Frequent name changes (BAMR → BNT) lost continuity in databases.
items.edge: Effectively a sponsor-backed alternative-asset insurance flywheel — generates ~$1.5B annual distributable earnings funneled into Brookfield-managed yield strategies, recreating the Athene/Apollo model at a discount to those peers. Just Group acquisition (UK retirement annuities) doubles AUM. Exchangeable into BN at 1:1 means it can never trade meaningfully below BN's per-share value — embedded floor.
catalysts: Convergence trade as US dual-listing mechanics get understood

items.name: Smithfield Foods, Inc.
items.thesis: Largest US pork producer carved-out from Hong Kong-listed WH Group via Nasdaq IPO/partial spin; ~88% still owned by WH Group, creating an orphan-like float despite a $11B mcap.
items.why: IPO priced below range ($20 vs $23-27 indicated) due to Chinese parent overhang and investor concerns about pork-cycle timing. WH Group still owns ~88% so float is small (~12%) and trades like a micro-cap despite the mcap. Most US food generalists don't include foreign-controlled food processors in their universe.
items.edge: Trades at ~10x P/E with 9.8% YoY revenue growth into 2025 ($15.5B), record FCF, and a $1.3B Sioux Falls capacity expansion announced; recent Nathan's Famous acquisition at 12.4x EBITDA is immediately accretive and signals disciplined capital allocation. Continued WH Group secondary sales will increase float and trigger index inclusion. Strong FCF supports buyback authorization.
catalysts: Continued WH Group secondary share sales
S&P MidCap inclusion as float grows
Margin recovery on hog cycle inflection

items.name: Resolute Holdings Management, Inc.
items.thesis: David Cote/Tom Knott-led alternative asset manager spun from CompoSecure to manage CMPO and future bolt-ons via long-term operating-services agreement — public 'Brookfield/3G-style' platform at sub-scale.
items.why: Tiny float (one share per 12 CMPO held), no revenue/profit at spin, taxable distribution drove indiscriminate selling. Early skepticism of fee-only platform with one client (CompoSecure). Massive run since spin (24x off initial $24 lows to $147) means most analysts now consider it 'too expensive', missing the optionality on adding new managed companies.
items.edge: David Cote (former Honeywell CEO) is one of the most credible operator-CEOs of his generation; Tom Knott has a strong CompoSecure track record. Vehicle is structured to acquire and operate businesses for fees + carry — early-stage alts platform analogous to Brookfield Asset Management or Constellation Software at a much smaller starting base. Each new managed acquisition compounds the fee base.
catalysts: Capital raise to fund managed-company acquisitions

items.name: EuroHoldings Ltd.
items.thesis: Greek shipping micro-cap spun from Euroseas with two older feeder containerships and no debt; immediately initiated strategic alternatives review and pivoted toward MR product tankers — pure NAV/SOTP arbitrage.
items.why: Spun off as a debt-free entity with two older containerships at a time of rising containership values, but at <$30M market cap is invisible to institutions. Greek-shipping orphan structure with confusing ticker history (ESEA→EHLD spin); strategic-alternatives review announcement six weeks after spin signals deeply discounted NAV.
items.edge: NAV substantially exceeds market cap based on second-hand vessel values plus cash; new pivot to product tankers (MR segment) acquired Hellas Avatar in late 2025 — aligns with 2026 product-tanker supply/demand inflection. Strategic alternatives review could result in special dividend, liquidation, or sale to peer. Asymmetric: small downside (vessel scrap value floor) with multi-bagger NAV upside.

items.name: Robin Energy Ltd.
items.thesis: Single-vessel Handysize tanker company spun from Toro Corp; same Petros Panagiotidis Greek-shipping family running both — hyper-micro-cap orphan trading near vessel value.
items.why: Toro shareholders received 1 RBNE share per 8 Toro shares — economically dilutive distribution that drove dumping. Started with literally one Handysize tanker; <$10M mcap so invisible. Same controlling shareholder as parent (Panagiotidis) creates governance discount.
items.edge: Vessel revenues grew 3.3x year-over-year through 2025 as Handysize charter rates strengthened; Q4 2025 vessel revenues hit $4.3M vs $1.3M prior year. Fleet expansion ongoing (Robin's 9-month 2025 vessel revenues $9.9M, up 45.6%). Trades near scrap+iron value with tanker upside; tiny float means any institutional interest moves price violently.
catalysts: Quarterly vessel revenue and fleet additions

items.name: Costamare Bulkers Holdings Limited
items.thesis: Pure-play dry-bulk shipping company spun from container-shipper Costamare; new vehicle inherits CMRE's dry-bulk fleet and trading platform.
items.why: CMRE holders are container-shipping investors who didn't want dry bulk exposure; 1-for-5 distribution ratio drove mass forced selling at the spin. Greek-shipping orphan with sub-$500M float makes it uninvestable for most institutions. Currently below NAV based on vessel values.
items.edge: Dry-bulk vessel values have rallied through 2025-2026 on China stimulus and grain trade rerouting; CMDB's fleet was contributed at conservative book values, leaving NAV optionality. Asset-light trading platform within CMDB drives capital-efficient earnings power separate from owned fleet. Same Konstantakopoulos family aligned alongside minority shareholders.
catalysts: Quarterly TCE rate and trading-platform updates

items.name: Rubico Inc.
items.thesis: Two modern Suezmax tankers (~157k DWT each, scrubber-equipped) spun from TOP Ships into a clean entity; nano-cap with current TCE rates plus IMO 2030 carbon-intensity tailwinds.
items.why: TOPS shareholders got Rubico in a tiny pro-rata distribution that immediately got dumped. Recent 1-for-10 reverse split reset price optics and likely scrubbed retail interest. Sub-$3M market cap is unfollowable but vessel NAV is ~$120M+ for two modern scrubber Suezmaxes — extreme NAV gap.
items.edge: Two Suezmax scrubber-equipped tankers with eco-design have premium TCE rates in any constructive crude-tanker market; 2026-2030 IMO carbon-intensity rules continue to advantage modern scrubber tonnage. NAV/share is dramatically higher than market price; any liquidity event (vessel sale, M&A combo, special dividend) is materially accretive. Lottery-ticket style asymmetry.

items.name: Canada Packers Inc.
items.thesis: Maple Leaf Foods' pork operations spun out into Canada Packers — North America's largest raised-without-antibiotics (RWA) pork producer with integrated supply chain, US-traded via OTC.
items.why: TSX-primary listing under 'CPKR' with US OTC pink ticker 'CPKRF' creates orphan-status — invisible to most US screens and excluded from Canadian small-cap benchmarks. Maple Leaf retained 16% so float is small. Pork-cycle bearish positioning post-Tyson and Smithfield troubles depresses valuation despite RWA differentiation.
items.edge: Premium 'raised without antibiotics' positioning attracts higher-value retail and foodservice contracts; integrated hog production through processing creates margin stability. Evergreen supply agreement with Maple Leaf provides downstream visibility. Trades at material discount to US-listed pork peers (SFD) on EBITDA. Capacity for capital returns once standalone reporting begins.
catalysts: Margin expansion as RWA pricing premium scales
Strategic interest from larger US/global pork players

items.name: AUMOVIO SE (ADR)
items.thesis: Continental's automotive electronics, sensors, ADAS, and software-defined vehicle business demerged into standalone AUMOVIO; €1.5B starting cash + €2.5B credit line; trades as US ADR (AMVOY).
items.why: European-listed (FRA: AUM) with US ADR creates dual-orphan status; Continental holders are tire/rubber investors who don't want auto-tech exposure. Valued at only €3.7B at launch despite €19.4B revenue (~0.2x sales) — depressed by general European auto pessimism. Slow-growth ADAS narrative ignores software-defined-vehicle inflection.
items.edge: Critical-content ADAS/sensors/SDV supplier to global OEMs at a time when those OEMs are racing to commodify and outsource the software stack — AUMOVIO has the IP and engineering to be the 'Tier 0.5' partner. Capacity to scale to €24B revenue, currently trading at <0.25x sales vs Aptiv at 1x+. Net cash gives flexibility for M&A or buybacks. Re-rating thesis on first standalone investor day.
catalysts: Index inclusion in DAX or SXXP

items.name: Liberty Live Holdings, Inc. (Series A)
items.thesis: Liberty's 30% Live Nation stake re-housed as a standalone holding company via split-off — eliminates tracking-stock NAV discount and unlocks direct ownership of LYV stake plus Quint and minority investments.
items.why: Tracking-stock conversion to asset-backed split-off completed mid-December 2025; many tax-loss sellers and ETF rebalancers dumped at year-end without considering the structural NAV discount narrowing. Even at $92, still trades at a material discount to LYV per-share value (look-through equity).
items.edge: Direct ownership of 69.6M Live Nation shares (~$22B at recent LYV pricing) means the embedded NAV is well above the current $8.6B market cap — historic Liberty discount of 25-35% for tracking stock should compress meaningfully now that it's a real cap-stock. John Malone's Liberty playbook has rewarded patient holders for decades. Buybacks and special distributions likely.
catalysts: Potential Greg Maffei capital allocation actions

items.name: Janus Living Inc.
items.thesis: First pure-play, fully RIDEA-structured publicly-traded senior-housing REIT carve-out from Healthpeak (DOC); ~10,400 units, $878M IPO proceeds, 85% still held by Healthpeak.
items.why: IPO/carve-out structure (rather than pure spin) created mixed institutional reception; Healthpeak retains ~85%, so float is small. Senior-housing sector still shadowed by COVID-era operating issues despite occupancy recovery. Newly created vehicle with limited trading history and no analyst coverage at launch.
items.edge: Only US-listed pure-play senior-housing REIT under fully RIDEA structure means upside from operating recovery flows directly to shareholders; sector has multi-year demographic tailwind from aging Baby Boomers. Recent Atlanta-area $240M acquisition demonstrates external growth runway. Healthpeak's gradual sell-down will boost float and trigger index inclusion. RIDEA structure should re-rate to NHC/SBRA premium.
catalysts: Healthpeak secondary share sales increasing float
First standalone earnings demonstrating same-property NOI growth
Continued external acquisitions at attractive cap rates

items.name: Twin Hospitality Group Inc.
items.thesis: Twin Peaks + Smokey Bones casual-dining spinoff from FAT Brands — now in Chapter 11 (April 2026) creating a deep distressed/post-bankruptcy equity stub setup.
items.why: Spun at $17.45, collapsed 99.5% over 12 months as same-store sales deteriorated and debt overhang took hold; subsequently filed Chapter 11. FAT Brands still owns ~95% so true float is tiny. Current micro-cap is essentially pricing zero recovery for equity post-restructuring — but Twin Peaks brand and ~85 units have real franchise value.
items.edge: Distressed-equity playbook: Chapter 11 reorganization will likely wipe out current equity but provides clean optionality on a recovery scenario where Twin Peaks brand is sold to PE or another franchisor at strategic multiples. Smokey Bones conversion runway adds optionality. For micro-cap distressed-debt funds, the post-emergence equity could be very interesting.
catalysts: FAT Brands strategic decision on residual stake

items.name: Grail, Inc.
items.thesis: Multi-cancer early-detection (MCED) liquid-biopsy company spun from Illumina under FTC/EU regulatory pressure; pure-play on Galleri commercial ramp with $1B+ Illumina-funded balance sheet at spin.
items.why: Forced spin from Illumina under regulatory pressure, not strategic logic, created indiscriminate selling on receipt; Illumina holders are sequencing-tools investors with no appetite for an early-stage diagnostic company. Galleri reimbursement/coverage timeline is uncertain, creating bear-case noise that drowns out commercial progress.
items.edge: Galleri (multi-cancer early-detection blood test) addresses a >$50B TAM with growing payer interest as coverage decisions land in 2026-2027; Illumina contributed $1B+ at spin to fund 4-5 years of cash burn. Strategic acquirers (large diagnostics companies, pharma with oncology screening) likely to circle if data continues to validate. Recent positive Medicare draft signals add asymmetric upside.

items.name: Solventum Corporation
items.thesis: 3M's healthcare business (medical, dental, health-info systems, purification) spun off into Solventum — leveraged carve-out with margin-expansion and divestiture optionality.
items.why: 3M's mass distribution to dividend/quality holders led to immediate selling pressure; high net leverage at spin (~3.5x) and the optical 'bad-cousin' label vs 3M's industrial purity scared off quality investors. Stagnant top-line growth narrative ignored the impending divestiture of low-growth purification business.
items.edge: Just announced sale of Purification & Filtration to Thermo Fisher for $4.1B — proceeds slash leverage, allow management to focus on core medical/dental segments where margins are 25%+. Standalone capital allocation, simplified portfolio, and ~$2B annual FCF support meaningful equity re-rating; consensus PT $80-90 vs current ~$70.
catalysts: Purification divestiture close and debt paydown
Multi-year revenue growth re-acceleration in medical surgical

items.name: Worthington Steel, Inc.
items.thesis: Steel processing spin from Worthington Enterprises — overlooked play on electrical-steel laminations for transformers and EV traction motors at a time of structural transformer shortage.
items.why: Worthington Enterprises (consumer/building products) holders sold the cyclical steel-processing stub; <$2B market cap is sub-scale for major steel funds (NUE/CLF/STLD). Recent quarter-by-quarter volatility from auto-cycle exposure obscures the multi-year electrical-steel growth story.
items.edge: Electrical-steel laminations for transformers and EV motors are structurally undersupplied (2-year transformer backlog cited by management); $85M Mexico + $85M Canada capacity additions come online 2026. Recent Sitem (Italy) acquisition adds European motor-lamination foothold. Trades at ~14x P/E vs strategic value of electrical-steel franchise — strategic-acquirer interest from Cleveland-Cliffs or NSC realistic.

items.name: Crane NXT, Co.
items.thesis: Spun from Crane Holdings to be the pure-play Payment Innovations + Security/Authentication Technologies business — niche industrial-tech compounder with hidden currency-printing utility.
items.why: Crane Holdings holders are diversified industrial investors who didn't want a payment/security niche stub; ongoing legacy-cash-handling pressure (declining cash usage in some markets) keeps generalist multiples compressed. Most recent quarter showed 19.5% revenue growth with non-GAAP EPS beating consensus, but stock still trades at ~13x earnings — well below industrial-tech peers.
items.edge: Federal Reserve 2026 print order projects banknote demand of 3.8-5.1 billion units — high-single-digit US currency revenue growth. Authentication and brand-protection segment is a high-margin oligopoly with growing TAM as luxury and pharmaceutical authentication scales. Active in tuck-in M&A; clean balance sheet supports buybacks. EV/EBITDA ~9x vs industrial-tech peers at 13-15x.

items.name: PHINIA Inc.
items.thesis: Fuel-systems and aftermarket business spun from BorgWarner; orphaned by EV-narrative funds despite strong recurring aftermarket FCF and commercial-vehicle/aftermarket exposure that's structurally insulated from EV transition.
items.why: BorgWarner holders rotated cleanly into the EV-pure-play parent; PHIN was viewed as 'stranded ICE asset' and dumped indiscriminately. Internal-combustion narrative blinds investors to the 15-20 year tail of diesel commercial-vehicles and the high-margin global aftermarket. Light analyst coverage relative to size.
items.edge: Aftermarket revenue is recurring high-margin, ~30% of business; commercial-vehicle and industrial customers will burn diesel for decades. ~$130M trailing net income on $3.5B revenue with capacity for buybacks and 2-3% dividend. Trades at ~9x forward EPS vs industrial peers at 12-15x. Capital-allocation discipline (buybacks + bolt-on M&A) closing the gap.
catalysts: Quarterly aftermarket revenue and margin updates
Strategic combination with another aftermarket player

items.name: Veralto Corporation
items.thesis: Danaher's Environmental & Applied Solutions segment (water analytics + product quality/marking) spun out — Danaher-quality compounder DNA at lower starting valuation, still under-followed.
items.why: Danaher holders kept the higher-multiple bioprocess parent and were ambivalent on the 'old industrial' E&AS stub; spun at a discount to peer ROP/BIO-Techne/IEX. Despite Danaher Business System DNA, market priced VLTO as a generic industrial-tools company.
items.edge: VLTO inherited Danaher's M&A playbook and capital-allocation rigor; water-quality segment has strong recurring-consumables economics and structural reshoring tailwinds. Product Quality & Innovation includes Videojet/X-Rite — high-margin franchises in adjacent spaces. Well below DHR-style multiple despite identical operating model. Even modest multiple expansion to peer levels = ~30% upside.
catalysts: Quarterly margin and DBS-driven productivity updates

items.name: WK Kellogg Co
items.thesis: North American cereal pure-play spun from Kellogg; subsequently agreed to be acquired by Ferrero — classic spin-then-merger value crystallization story still playing out.
items.why: Kellogg holders rotated to global snacks (Kellanova/K); KLG was treated as a declining North American cereal stub trading at ~5x EBITDA. Ferrero deal (announced 2025) changed the calculus but spread to deal price reflects ongoing antitrust risk and timing uncertainty.
items.edge: Ferrero deal at $23/share provides hard deal-price floor; antitrust process is expected to close 2H 2026 with high probability of approval. Standalone, brands like Frosted Flakes, Froot Loops have demonstrated pricing power. For arb funds, the spread-to-deal is attractive risk/reward; for fundamental holders, the deal validates intrinsic value the public market wouldn't recognize.
catalysts: Possible bidding war if Mars or other strategic emerges

items.name: Atlanta Braves Holdings, Inc.
items.thesis: Atlanta Braves MLB franchise + Battery Atlanta mixed-use development split off from Liberty Media — only pure-play publicly traded MLB team trading at material discount to franchise value.
items.why: Tracking stock conversion to a real-asset stub created confused float; baseball/sports franchise valuation methods aren't standard among generalist equity funds. MLB media-rights uncertainty and team-payroll concerns weighed on multiples. Mixed-use real estate development underwrites partly hidden by sports-team cash-flow noise.
items.edge: Recent MLB franchise sales (Orioles ~$1.7B, Twins talks ~$1.7B) imply Braves franchise alone is worth $3-4B; The Battery Atlanta mixed-use complex adds another $500M-$800M of real-asset value. Combined SOTP suggests $4-5B+ NAV vs $3B mcap. Liberty/Malone has historically rewarded patient holders with eventual sale or special distribution. Tax-efficient sale to billionaire buyer remains the bull-case endgame.
catalysts: Eventual sale of franchise to billionaire bidder

items.name: SBC Medical Group Holdings Inc.
items.thesis: Japan-based aesthetic-medicine MSO carved out via SPAC/listing process onto Nasdaq; franchise services across cosmetic surgery, dermatology, fertility — global expansion underway.
items.why: Listed via SPAC merger / carve-out path with HeartCore Go-IPO consulting structure — confusing legal heritage for US institutional investors. Japan-domiciled with majority foreign-controlled ownership creates governance discount. Aesthetic medicine viewed as discretionary, not staple — over-discounted in current macro.
items.edge: Largest aesthetic-medicine MSO in Japan with mid-teens revenue growth and high-30s EBITDA margins; expanding into US and Asia franchise base. $50M shelf + buyback announced shows management confidence. As foreign-issuer reporting normalizes and US analyst coverage initiates, multiple expansion likely. Profitable medical-services compounder rarely stays this cheap.
catalysts: Possible secondary listing on Tokyo or Singapore
items.parent: Pono Capital / SBC Medical Japan parent

items.name: Sandoz Group AG (ADR)
items.thesis: Global generics + biosimilars leader spun from Novartis; trades on SIX as primary listing with US ADR (SDZNY) on OTCQX — index-orphan on US side and structurally undervalued.
items.why: Listed on SIX Switzerland with only OTCQX ADR in the US — excluded from US benchmarks (S&P 500, Russell). Novartis ADR holders received Sandoz ADRs but most sold immediately to remain in pure innovative-Pharma exposure. Generics narrative is bearish despite strong biosimilars TAM growth.
items.edge: Biosimilars business has multi-year tailwind from Humira/Enbrel/Stelara biosimilar launches across global markets; pricing structures more durable than 'commodity generics' bear case suggests. Strong cash flow funding deleveraging plus dividend; >100% stock appreciation since spin yet still trades at <12x forward EPS vs branded-pharma peers at 15-18x. Catalysts on US listing and index inclusion would re-rate.

items.name: Knife River Corporation
items.thesis: Aggregates and construction-materials business spun from MDU Resources — pure infrastructure/IIJA play with regional pricing power on aggregates, sand, gravel, and ready-mix.
items.why: MDU utility/dividend holders sold the cyclical construction-materials stub; original spin valuation reflected pre-IIJA-ramp expectations. Acquisition-driven growth strategy (buying regional aggregates operators) was viewed skeptically until the deals proved accretive.
items.edge: Regional aggregates business has near-monopoly pricing in many western US markets; IIJA + state-and-local infrastructure capex are multi-year tailwinds materializing through 2026-2028. M&A roll-up of regional players accretive to per-share earnings; strong FCF generation funds both buybacks and continued bolt-ons. Strategic-acquirer interest from CRH/Vulcan/Martin Marietta is realistic given concentration of attractive pits.
catalysts: Quarterly aggregates pricing and volume updates

items.name: GE HealthCare Technologies Inc.
items.thesis: Imaging + ultrasound + patient-monitoring + pharmaceutical-diagnostics pure-play spun from GE; #1 in MR/CT imaging globally with multi-year backlog.
items.why: First leg of GE breakup, distributed to a broad GE shareholder base that included many non-healthcare-focused funds; sold-out indiscriminately at spin. Hospital capex pause narratives over-discounted long-term imaging-volume growth. Smaller analyst following relative to peers SYK/MDT/PHG.
items.edge: Backlog of over $19B with improving book-to-bill; AI-enabled imaging products driving share gains. PDx (pharma diagnostics) segment is high-margin recurring contrast-agent business. Margin expansion ongoing as standalone management drives portfolio rationalization. Trades at meaningful discount to medical-device peers despite market-leading positions and accelerating procedure volumes.

items.name: Kellanova
items.thesis: Global snacking + international cereal + plant-based + frozen foods (Pringles, Cheez-It, Eggo) — agreed to be acquired by Mars at $83.50 cash; arb spread + closing optionality.
items.why: Mars deal announced at $83.50 cash provides hard ceiling; spread to deal reflects antitrust uncertainty (especially in snack-bar/cracker overlap). Some legacy Kellogg holders had to sell on receipt due to mandate restrictions on foreign-controlled or PE-controlled investments.
items.edge: Mars deal is materially advanced through US/EU antitrust review and on track to close mid-2026; deal arbitrage offers attractive annualized yield to close. Standalone, Kellanova has demonstrated mid-single-digit organic growth and pricing power post-COVID. Even if deal breaks, fundamental value supports current trading price. Asymmetric risk/reward favors holders.
catalysts: Standalone performance updates if deal extends

items.name: Atlanta Braves Holdings, Inc. (Series C)
items.thesis: Series C non-voting class of Atlanta Braves Holdings; trades at a small discount to BATRA (Series A voting) — pair-trade or value-conscious-entry alternative for the same MLB franchise SOTP thesis.
items.why: Two share classes (BATRA voting + BATRK non-voting) create persistent small spread that retail/smaller institutions don't arbitrage; non-voting structure depresses BATRK relative to BATRA despite identical economic interest. Same underlying tracking-stock-conversion forced selling drove both lower than franchise NAV.
items.edge: Identical economic exposure to Atlanta Braves franchise + Battery Atlanta as BATRA, but at a slight discount; in a take-private scenario, both classes would receive same per-share consideration — discount disappears. Same MLB franchise NAV thesis ($4-5B+) applies. Larger float in BATRK provides better liquidity for institutional sizing.
catalysts: Same as BATRA: MLB franchise sale comps, Battery monetization, eventual sale
Voting/non-voting class collapse if take-private occurs

items.name: Liberty Live Holdings, Inc. (Series C)
items.thesis: Non-voting Series C of Liberty Live Holdings; same Live Nation 30% stake exposure as LLYVA at modest discount — pair-trade and SOTP play.
items.why: Two-class spin structure creates persistent small spread; LLYVK (Series C, non-voting) trades at slight discount to LLYVA despite same economic interest. Recent split-off completed December 2025 still in 'forced selling' window from former Liberty Media holders.
items.edge: Direct exposure to 69.6M Live Nation shares (~$22B) at a holdco that trades meaningfully below look-through NAV; LLYVK provides marginally cheaper entry to the same compounding thesis. Larger float vs LLYVA provides better liquidity for institutional sized positions. Buybacks, special distributions, or eventual collapse of dual-class structure are all upside catalysts.
catalysts: Same as LLYVA: buybacks, Live Nation outperformance, special distributions
Index inclusion as post-spin seasoning completes

activists.title: B. ACTIVE ACTIVIST CAMPAIGNS
activists.blurb: Credible activist 13D filings, stock not yet re-rated

items.name: Tripadvisor, Inc.
items.thesis: Activist sees TheFork + Viator alone worth ~$2.5B vs whole-company EV of ~$1.3B; pushing for asset sale or full sale.
items.why: March 22, 2026 cooperation agreement: board expanded 8 to 10; Andrew F. Cates and Dhiren R. Fonseca appointed; Starboard can recommend two more. Strategic review of TheFork in process. BofA upgraded to Buy with $15 PT.
items.edge: Stock still trades at ~$11.39 vs sum-of-parts >$15-20/share per Starboard; control overhang from Liberty TripAdvisor complicates sale optics, keeping institutional money sidelined.

items.name: BILL Holdings, Inc.
items.thesis: SMB payments leader at trough valuation; Starboard pushing for sale or majority board overhaul.
items.why: Starboard launched coordinated proxy campaign; February 2026 letter slammed sustained underperformance and board inaction; March 2026 incremental cooperation discussions ongoing; stock has not re-rated meaningfully.
items.edge: Stock still ~70% below 2021 highs, trades at ~3x sales vs peers at 6-8x; market still skeptical of payments TAM and embedded finance narrative; sale process not yet formal.
catalysts: Fed rate cuts driving SMB volume

items.name: Six Flags Entertainment Corporation
items.thesis: Cedar Fair / Six Flags merger has failed to deliver synergies; activist pushing for full sale of company.
items.why: March 17, 2026 escalation letter demanding immediate sale process; Travis Kelce became brand ambassador; board response pending. Stock jumped ~9% on the letter but hasn't re-rated to a sale-process valuation.
items.edge: $5.2B debt load is scaring buyers; market doesn't yet believe a sale is achievable; PE buyers (Six Flags was previously owned by Apollo) could re-emerge; trades at ~6x EBITDA vs peers at 8-10x.
catalysts: Potential PE bid from Apollo, Blackstone, or KKR
items.activist: JANA Partners (with Travis Kelce, Glenn Murphy, Dave Habiger)

items.name: Fiserv, Inc.
items.thesis: Payments leader stock down ~67% in 2025 on execution issues; activist supports new CEO turnaround and pushes for non-core divestitures.
items.why: JANA built ~2.25M share, $150M position in Q4 2025; publicly disclosed Feb 17, 2026 with dialogue underway. Mike Lyons hired as CEO; 2026 framed as transition year. No formal cooperation agreement yet.
items.edge: Stock still down ~12% YTD on top of 67% 2025 collapse; market hasn't priced in any successful turnaround; trades at ~12x EPS vs historical ~20x and peer Fidelity National at 14x. Capital return + divestiture optionality.

items.name: Avantor, Inc.
items.thesis: Lab supply leader trading at $8.52 vs Engine intrinsic value $17-19 (sale) or $22-26 (standalone turnaround).
items.why: Engine Capital escalated August 2025; new CEO Emmanuel Ligner appointed (effective Aug 18, 2025) with $400M cost transformation through 2027. Stock has bounced from lows but still trades at ~50% of 2024 levels.
items.edge: Stock still down 32-42% YTD/12mo; market skeptical of cost program execution; deep value at sub-$9 vs Engine sale value of $17-19; high leverage discounted but FCF inflection ahead.
catalysts: Potential PE buyout (sponsors like Carlyle / KKR familiar with sector)

items.name: Monro, Inc.
items.thesis: Auto-repair chain at trough; Icahn (largest shareholder at 15%) pursuing operational + strategic alternatives; LBO/sale optionality.
items.why: Icahn filed 13D Nov 5, 2025; Monro responded with poison pill (rights plan) to prevent takeover. Closing 145 underperforming locations. Brett Icahn / Icahn organization likely to seek board representation.
items.edge: Small/illiquid microcap (~$540M); poison pill creates standoff; consumer trade-down hurting near-term but tire replacement is non-discretionary; private market value (1,100 shops, real estate) likely above public value.
catalysts: Strategic review or sale process initiation

items.name: WEX Inc.
items.thesis: Fleet/payments fintech under-earning at sub-$175; Impactive nominating directors with fintech expertise to drive operational reset.
items.why: 13D filed Feb 9, 2026 disclosing 5.0% and four nominees. WEX reduced board to 9 directors, prompting Impactive to drop one nominee. Investor presentation released April 14, 2026 outlining case for change. Active proxy contest.
items.edge: Stock still ~40% below 2023 highs; market skeptical of EV/fleet payments transition risk; activist proxy contest creates near-term re-rating catalyst regardless of outcome; PE buyout precedent in sector.

items.name: Riot Platforms, Inc.
items.thesis: Bitcoin miner with 1.7GW power capacity; Starboard sees $1.6B EBITDA potential pivoting to AI/HPC data centers like CoreWeave/Iris peers.
items.why: Starboard built 12.7M share position (Q4 2024); Feb 18, 2026 letter pushed harder on AI pivot. AMD signed 10-yr/$311M Rockdale lease in Jan 2026. Stock has not closed valuation gap to AI data center comps (CRWV trading at much richer EV/MW).
items.edge: RIOT trades at ~$4M/MW of capacity vs AI data center comps at $20-40M/MW; market still values it as bitcoin miner not data center. Catalyst: more hyperscaler leases.
catalysts: Potential REIT or yieldco spin-off of power assets

items.name: Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc.
items.thesis: Activist opposes Netflix offer for WBD assets, backs Paramount bid, threatens proxy fight if WBD board doesn't reconsider.
items.why: Ancora built $200M stake by Feb 2026; publicly opposing Netflix transaction; shareholder vote expected by April 2026 on Netflix deal. Paramount sweetening bid. Board accountability campaign on track.
items.edge: Stock at $27.47 reflects Netflix deal value; if Paramount bid prevails (or auction develops), stock could re-rate to mid-$30s. Even if Netflix deal closes, post-transaction stub (cable/networks) is deeply discounted.
catalysts: April 2026 shareholder vote on Netflix deal
Possible Comcast / Apple / private equity entry

items.name: First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc.
items.thesis: Industrial REIT trading at >100bps cap rate spread / NAV discount to peers; Litt nominates self for board, demands $500M-$1B asset sales.
items.why: Litt sent letter Feb 26, 2026 with detailed governance critique. ~$73 NAV vs $63.75 stock = 15-20% upside thesis. Active proxy contest ahead of 2026 annual meeting.
items.edge: Industrial REITs out of favor due to data center capex cycle and warehouse oversupply concerns; market hasn't priced in any sale process or NAV gap closure; FR is digestible take-private size for KKR/Blackstone/Brookfield.

items.name: National Health Investors, Inc.
items.thesis: Senior housing REIT with structural NAV discount; Land & Buildings pushing board accountability and senior housing recovery thesis.
items.why: Proxy fight ongoing. Senior housing fundamentals improving (occupancy recovery + rate cuts), but board governance issues persist. Q1 2026 dividend declared. Earnings call upcoming.
items.edge: REIT space broadly out of favor; senior housing operating recovery story not fully priced in; activist creates governance optionality. Trades at ~13x AFFO vs senior housing peers at 16-18x.
catalysts: Potential M&A in senior housing REIT space

items.name: Performance Food Group Company
items.thesis: Foodservice distributor merger candidate with US Foods; Sachem Head pushing the deal as Ferguson knows both boards.
items.why: Cooperation agreement: Scott Ferguson joined PFGC board Sept 23, 2025 (audit & finance committee overseeing M&A). PFGC + USFD signed clean team agreement to facilitate info sharing for potential combination. FTC review unclear.
items.edge: Median analyst PT $116.50 vs $93.50 spot; market skeptical of FTC approval for #2/#3 foodservice combination; merger arb opportunity if announced. Standalone PFGC also undervalued at ~10x EBITDA.
catalysts: Definitive merger announcement with US Foods
Potential PE LBO if regulatory blocked

items.name: Lamb Weston Holdings, Inc.
items.thesis: Frozen potato leader; cooperation gave 6 new directors; new exec chair Jan Craps installed Feb 2026 to drive turnaround. Stock still suppressed.
items.why: Cooperation agreement reached; 6 new directors added; Jan Craps (former Budweiser APAC CEO) hired as Executive Chair Feb 2026. JANA still pressing for sale process if turnaround stalls. New CFO appointed.
items.edge: Stock collapsed from $115 in 2023 to $44.82 on ERP issues + frozen potato oversupply; supply rationalization underway; trades at <8x normalized EBITDA vs peers at 11-13x. Sale optionality remains live.
catalysts: Potential sale process if Craps turnaround stalls

items.name: Fox Factory Holding Corp.
items.thesis: Bike/UTV/truck suspension leader down 73% peak-to-trough; Engine secured 2 board seats Feb 2026 with margin recovery roadmap.
items.why: Cooperation agreement Feb 2026: Bazaar + 1 other joining board; Chairman Mendenhall and director Waitman retiring at 2026 annual meeting. Transformation Committee established.
items.edge: Stock collapsed 73% from peak; aftermarket bike/UTV cycles bottoming; OEM (Ford/GM) truck suspension recovery story; activist committee creates execution accountability. Trades at <6x normalized EBITDA.

items.name: Adtalem Global Education Inc.
items.thesis: For-profit education leader with strong execution; activists pushing back on $1.5B Walden U acquisition as value-destructive.
items.why: Engine + Hawk Ridge open letter Oct 2025 publicly opposed deal. Adtalem authorized $750M buyback Dec 15, 2025. Investor Day Feb 24, 2026. Company still pushing forward with Walden integration.
items.edge: Adtalem stock has rallied on standalone results (Q2 EPS +35%) but Walden overhang persists; if deal terminated, buyback acceleration drives further upside; if deal closes, market still discounts ~$25/share of accretion.
catalysts: Walden deal close or termination decision

items.name: Matthews International Corporation
items.thesis: Diversified industrial; Barington's second proxy fight in two years; company executing on asset sales (SGK $350M, Warehouse $230M).
items.why: Jan 15, 2026 cooperation agreement: Barington withdrew nominations under standstill. Matthews already sold SGK Brand Solutions for $350M and Warehouse Automation for $230M. Memorialization business + Industrial segments remain.
items.edge: Microcap (~$857M); divestiture proceeds being deployed; pure-play memorialization businesses trade at higher multiples; sum-of-parts likely north of $40/share vs $27.54 spot.
catalysts: Use of proceeds from completed divestitures

items.name: Universal Technical Institute, Inc.
items.thesis: Trade school operator with insider activist (Shackelton is also Chairman); accumulating at $23-25 in Dec 2025 signals strong continued conviction.
items.why: Coliseum bought additional shares Dec 1-5, 2025 at $23.66-$25.38. Stock has run to $38.25 since but Coliseum/Shackelton position is conviction-signaling. Dallas campus expansion announced Oct 2025.
items.edge: Trade school sector benefiting from skilled labor shortage + AI-driven white-collar disruption; UTI under-followed; insider/activist alignment unique. Still trades at <12x EBITDA vs sector at 14x.
catalysts: Potential additional M&A or capital return

items.name: Whitestone REIT
items.thesis: Sun Belt strip-center REIT with persistent NAV discount; ousted founder Mastandrea launching full-board replacement proxy fight to drive sale or liquidation.
items.why: Mastandrea announced campaign Jan 2 / Jan 5, 2026. Formally nominated six candidates March 2026. Cites portfolio shrinkage from 53 to 50 properties since 2021. Active full-board replacement campaign.
items.edge: Microcap REIT (~$986M) with insider founder activism = unusual setup; Sun Belt grocery-anchored strip is a coveted asset class; private market value likely 25-35% above stock; full board overhaul signals real catalyst.

items.name: Lyft, Inc.
items.thesis: #2 rideshare with persistent valuation gap to Uber; Engine driving capital allocation and dual-class collapse story.
items.why: Engine campaign March 2025; nominated 2 directors April 16, 2025 then withdrew after Lyft committed to capital return. Significant share repurchases announced. Dual-class collapse not yet achieved.
items.edge: Trades at ~$15 vs Uber at much richer multiple; AV (Waymo/Tesla) overhang depressed sentiment; standalone capital return + potential M&A target (Amazon, DoorDash, etc.) creates re-rating optionality.

items.name: V.F. Corporation
items.thesis: Apparel conglomerate (Vans, North Face, Timberland, Dickies) trading at ~$21 vs sum-of-parts $40+; turnaround under CEO Bracken Darrell with activist board representation.
items.why: Engaged Capital won 2 board seats; founding family heirs publicly backed Engaged. CEO Bracken Darrell executing 'Reinvent' plan with cost cuts and brand divestitures (Supreme sold for $1.5B in 2024). Stock still suppressed pending Vans inflection.
items.edge: Stock down ~70% from peak; market still skeptical of Vans recovery despite stabilization signals; sum-of-parts (especially North Face standalone valuation) is materially above consolidated multiple. Real estate optionality.

items.name: Honeywell International Inc.
items.thesis: Industrial conglomerate breaking into three independent listed companies (Aerospace, Automation, Advanced Materials) under Elliott pressure, separation expected H2 2026.
items.why: Honeywell announced Feb 6, 2025 it will split into three independent listed companies. Elliott deepened cooperation May 2025 ahead of breakup. Separation targeted H2 2026 and will be tax-free to shareholders. Elliott's largest-ever single-stock position at $5B+.
items.edge: Sum-of-parts (aerospace alone could re-rate to LMT/RTX multiple) materially above current $233 stub; automation/materials spinoffs create forced sellers and downstream catalysts; near-term separation unlocks value pre-execution.
catalysts: H2 2026 separation completion (advanced materials + aerospace + automation)

items.name: Phillips 66
items.thesis: Refining/midstream conglomerate; Elliott won 2 board seats in May 2025 proxy fight, continues pushing for spin of midstream assets and CPChem stake monetization.
items.why: May 2025 proxy contest: Elliott won 2 of 4 contested seats (Sigmund Cornelius, Michael Heim). Q2/Q3 2025 13F shows Elliott built to ~$2.5B position. Strategic review of midstream remains live; PSX still resisting full break-up.
items.edge: Stock trades at conglomerate discount despite Elliott board presence; midstream spin (potentially worth $30B+ standalone) not yet announced; CPChem (50% JV with Chevron) sale could unlock $10B+; refining recovery into 2026.

items.name: PepsiCo, Inc.
items.thesis: Beverage/snack giant under-earning vs. KO; Elliott $4B activist campaign secured Dec 2025 deal to cut 20% of SKUs and refranchise bottlers.
items.why: Elliott letter Sept 2, 2025 detailing $4B stake. Dec 8, 2025 PepsiCo agreed with Elliott to cut nearly 20% of products by early 2026. Refranchising and portfolio review continue. Stock has not fully re-rated to KO multiple.
items.edge: Trades at ~17x EPS vs. KO at 23x; refranchising could unlock $20B of capital and lift margins; Elliott is methodical and persistent; near-term beats from cost reset.

items.name: Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company
items.thesis: Hybrid cloud / networking play; Elliott took $1.5B stake April 2025, secured Strategy Committee + Bob Calderoni board seat to drive AI server / Juniper integration value.
items.why: Elliott took >$1.5B position April 2025. July 16, 2025 cooperation agreement: HPE added Bob Calderoni (KLA Chairman) to board; new Strategy Committee formed. CEO Antonio Neri remains under pressure to deliver Juniper synergies. AI server momentum builds.
items.edge: Trades at 9x EPS vs. peers at 15-20x; Juniper integration synergies ($600M target) plus AI server tailwinds underappreciated; Elliott historically pushes CEO changes if execution disappoints; sub-$30 stock has 40%+ upside.

items.name: Pinterest, Inc.
items.thesis: Visual discovery platform; Elliott $1B convert at $22.72 strike to fund $2B accelerated buyback; AI commerce monetization thesis.
items.why: March 3, 2026 Elliott invested $1B in convertible senior notes (1.75% coupon, 2031 maturity, $22.72 strike = 30% premium). PINS to use proceeds for $1B ASR. Board upsized buyback authorization to $3.5B. Stock currently below convert strike.
items.edge: Trades at <$21 vs $22.72 convert strike (Elliott is arguably underwriting downside); H1 2026 buyback aggression provides EPS accretion; AI commerce + Amazon partnership early innings; Elliott on board since 2022.
catalysts: Potential additional Elliott escalation if stock languishes

items.name: Equinix, Inc.
items.thesis: Data center REIT; Elliott pushing to amplify AI/HPC positioning vs CoreWeave/CRWV given Equinix's superior interconnection density.
items.why: Elliott disclosed initial 0.15% position Q1 2025, materially increased Q2 2025. CNBC reported August 2025 Elliott has data center expertise from Cyxtera/CoreWeave; observers expect board seat invite before May 2026 AGM. EQIX management collaborative.
items.edge: AI capex cycle + interconnection density makes EQIX premier data center, but trades at lower EV/MW than pure AI plays; Elliott catalyst could re-rate even further; activist tailwind into May 2026 AGM.

items.name: Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd.
items.thesis: #3 cruise line; Elliott took >10% stake in Feb 2026, secured massive board overhaul (5 of 9 directors replaced) with industry heavyweights including Steve Pagliuca and Alex Cruz.
items.why: March 27, 2026 cooperation agreement: 5 new directors (Alex Cruz ex-British Airways CEO, Kevin Lansberry ex-Disney CFO, Brian MacDonald CDK Global CEO, Jonathan Cohen Hepco CEO, Steve Pagliuca Bain Capital). 4 long-time directors stepped down March 31. Chidsey appointed Chairman.
items.edge: Trades at ~$21 vs RCL multiple gap (>30% discount on EV/EBITDA); 2026 muted guidance overhang creates entry; Pagliuca/Bain involvement signals potential PE bid; cruise demand strong into 2026/2027.
catalysts: Potential PE/strategic interest given Bain involvement

items.name: Kenvue Inc.
items.thesis: J&J consumer health spinoff (Tylenol, Listerine); Starboard activism led to KMB $48.7B acquisition deal announced Nov 3, 2025.
items.why: Starboard built sizeable stake H2 2024-H1 2025; Jeff Smith joined board March 2025. Kimberly-Clark announced $48.7B EV acquisition Nov 3, 2025. Deal expected to close 2026 - merger arb situation.
items.edge: Stock at $17.61 vs Kimberly-Clark deal value ($48.7B EV / ~$21/share cash + stock implied); merger arb spread reflects regulatory + KMB share price risk; downside protected by Starboard's strategic review optionality if deal breaks.

items.name: Pfizer Inc.
items.thesis: Big pharma post-COVID hangover; Starboard $1B activist position pushing turnaround under CEO Bourla, board refresh.
items.why: Starboard built ~$1B position late 2024. Stopped short of nominating directors before Jan 25, 2026 deadline. Could revisit at 2026 annual meeting if engagement stalls. Pfizer 2026 framed as transition with Seagen ramp + LOE management.
items.edge: Trades at $27.56 vs $40+ pre-COVID levels; sum-of-parts including Seagen oncology + Haleon stake materially above stub value; Starboard pressure has prevented complacency; pipeline assets (oncology, obesity) underappreciated.

items.name: Keurig Dr Pepper Inc.
items.thesis: Coffee + soft drinks conglomerate; Starboard pushing for clarity on JDE Peet acquisition and value-creation plan post-merger.
items.why: Starboard publicly known to be engaged October 2025 ahead of JDE Peet deal close. Nomination deadline Feb 2026 passed without slate, suggesting amicable resolution underway. Stock has lagged peers significantly.
items.edge: Trades at $26.53 vs sum-of-parts >$35; JDE Peet deal initially poorly received, creating dislocation; Starboard's coffee experience (it pushed Whitbread/Costa Coffee value) suggests potential break-up thesis post-close.
catalysts: JDE Peet deal close + integration milestones

items.name: Align Technology, Inc.
items.thesis: Invisalign maker down 70% from peak; Elliott pushing for cost discipline, capital return, and turnaround under new strategy.
items.why: Elliott took activist stake in Align Technology (ALGN) disclosed Sept 2025. Stock down ~70% from 2021 highs. Engagement ongoing; details of formal demands not yet public but Elliott known for board pressure.
items.edge: Trades at $190 vs $750 2021 peak; 12x EPS vs medical device peers at 25x; Elliott activist tailwind + clear orthodontic TAM growth; insider buying signals confidence; potential PE/strategic acquirer interest.

items.name: Solventum Corporation

items.edge: Stock at $70 vs Trian PT $140 by 2027 = 100% upside thesis; spinoff dyssynergies + post-spin debt overhang creating discount; 3M Health legacy 2x organic growth + higher margins than parent; activist accelerant.

items.name: Janus Henderson Group plc

items.name: CVS Health Corporation

items.edge: Stock at $77 vs sum-of-parts $100+ (Aetna + Caremark + Pharmacy + Oak Street); Aetna margins recovering off 2024 trough; strong FCF for buybacks; Glenview's operational expertise on board ensures execution accountability.

items.name: The Cooper Companies, Inc.

items.name: Teleflex Incorporated

March 27, 2026 Irenic went public with letter accusing board of refusing to engage with credible acquisition interest. Teleflex defending standalone strategy with $1B buyback + restructuring.

items.name: Snap Inc.

items.name: CoStar Group, Inc.
items.thesis: Real estate data; D.E. Shaw seeking majority board control to halt Homes.com $3B+ value destruction. Third Point exited but D.E. Shaw escalating.

items.name: Euronet Worldwide, Inc.

items.edge: Stock at $74.40 vs Voss $115-140 PT = 55-90% upside; Atleos comp suggests ATM business standalone worth $4-5B; remittance + epay segments materially undervalued; spin or full sale unlocks gap.

items.name: Surgery Partners, Inc.

items.name: Sturm, Ruger & Company, Inc.

Possible re-emergence of $4.80 (or higher) buyout offer

items.name: Civeo Corporation

items.edge: Stock at $30.18 vs Engine $47 standalone (4-5x EBITDA) target; activist board seats ensure execution; commodity tailwinds (Australian iron ore + Canadian oil sands) into 2026; potential PE/strategic acquirer interest.

items.name: BlackRock ESG Capital Allocation Term Trust

items.edge: Stock trades at ~12-15% discount to NAV; if Saba wins majority, tender at NAV (or conversion to open-end) crystallizes the gap; even if Saba loses, settlement likely includes large tender offer (BlackRock pattern with BIGZ/BMEZ).

items.name: BlackRock Capital Allocation Term Trust

items.name: BlackRock Health Sciences Term Trust

items.name: Exor N.V. (Agnelli family holding)

If Bollore loses appeal it must launch a tender within 6 months at a 'fair price' that arbitrators argue should be ~NAV. Either tender or continued forced asset sales narrow the discount.

Asset sales (further trims of Telecom Italia, sale of Banijay legacy stake)

Possible Boardwalk re-IPO when rates drop
